main_summary.summary: Rick Tuttle, interviewed in Culver City, California on April 11, 2013 by David Cline for the Southern Oral History Program with the Smithsonian NMAAHC and Library of Congress, traces a civil rights trajectory from a New England Civil War family lineage and an early childhood encounter with NAACP-organized Pullman porters through fraternity desegregation at Wesleyan University, graduate study at UCLA, and direct participation in SNCC and SCLC voter-registration and desegregation work in 1963. Recruited by Steve McNichols after reading a John Poppy article on Sam Block, Charles McLaurin, and Willie Wazir Peacock, Tuttle worked the COFO office in Greenwood, Mississippi during the murder of Medgar Evers and the Winona jailing of Fannie Lou Hamer, Lawrence Guyot, June Johnson, and Annelle Ponder. He was reassigned to Atlanta, then to Savannah with Hosea Williams and Benjamin Van Clark, where he was arrested under Judge Victor Mulling's seven-piece-bond detention and held in the Chatham County Jail for roughly six weeks until attorney Aaron Buchsbaum and Dr. William Waxler secured his release. Subsequent work included the UCLA Student Coordinating Committee, the No on Proposition 14 fair-housing campaign, the RFK 1968 campaign, the Tom Bradley 1973 mayoral coalition, four terms as Los Angeles City Controller, private-club desegregation, South Africa divestment, and redlining disclosure ordinances.
key_themes: SNCC and SCLC field organizing in Mississippi and Georgia 1963
White ally jail risk and the institutional protection of federal-family ties
Cross-coalition liberal-progressive electoral politics in Los Angeles
Continuity from civil rights work to fair-housing, anti-apartheid, and gender/orientation equity
Voting Rights Act preservation and the long arc of movement institutions
main_summary.historical_significance:
The interview is a primary-source account of the COFO Greenwood office during the exact week of the Medgar Evers assassination and the Winona jail beatings, with Tuttle's direct phone call to Governor Ross Barnett and his attribution of that conversation to the eventual release of Hamer, Guyot, Ponder, and June Johnson. It documents the Savannah movement under Hosea Williams and Benjamin Van Clark from the position of a jailed white volunteer, including the Chatham County Jail trustees who physically protected Tuttle, the Aaron Buchsbaum bail intervention, and the suppressed Atlanta Constitution communist-smear story that was killed by Stanley Mosk's letter and Frank Cherry's call to the publisher.

It also traces continuity from the 1963 Mississippi-Georgia experience to the UCLA Student Coordinating Committee, the No on Proposition 14 referendum, the RFK 1968 California campaign, the Tom Bradley 1973 mayoral coalition, and Tuttle's four terms as Los Angeles City Controller, during which he led the private-club desegregation ordinance and the South Africa divestment affidavit requirement. The 2008 return tour with his second wife Rebecca to Greenwood, Canton, and Savannah adds reflective coda material on the persistence of Delta poverty and the institutional memory of the movement.

chapters.title: New England Roots and a Porter's NAACP Card
chapters.summary: Tuttle opens with his paternal family's arrival in New Haven in 1638 and his great-grandfather Monroe Gillette's Civil War service at Fort Wagner and Petersburg. He recounts a formative childhood memory of his father using an NAACP card to enlist Pullman porters in helping the family transfer trains in Cincinnati after the war, framing organized solidarity as the lesson that shaped his later political life.

chapters.title: Wesleyan and the Fraternity Desegregation Fight
chapters.summary: Tuttle attends Wesleyan University beginning fall 1957 and becomes president of Alpha Chi Rho, which discovers a Christian-only clause and a hidden whites-only clause in its national charter. With Stephen S. Trott and Bruce Corwin as the precipitating members, the chapter breaks from the national after a one-year ultimatum. Tuttle takes a junior year off in 1960 to read and reflect during the sit-in wave.

chapters.title: John Maguire, William Sloane Coffin, and the Freedom Rides
chapters.summary: Wesleyan religion instructor John Maguire receives a phone call from Rev. William Sloane Coffin Jr after the Anniston bus burning and joins the Freedom Rides with department chair David Swift following. Tuttle and his roommate decide to go but the bus has already left. He frames the Freedom Rides as a strategic exploitation of interstate Commerce Clause jurisdiction.

chapters.title: UCLA, Steve McNichols, and the Decision to Go South
chapters.summary: Tuttle arrives at UCLA in 1962 to study under Progressive Era historian George Mowry. He meets Steve McNichols, NSA representative and former Houston Freedom Rider, and his circle of West Coast civil rights veterans including Bob and Helen Berry Singleton and Robert C. Farrell. A John Poppy article on SNCC's Sam Block, Charles McLaurin, and Willie Wazir Peacock in the Mississippi Delta catalyzes Tuttle's decision to go South.

chapters.title: George E. Brown's Letter and Arrival in Greenwood
chapters.summary: At a Hollywood Hills party celebrating the election of Congressman George E. Brown Jr and councilman Tom Bradley, Tuttle is introduced to the Californians for Liberal Representation network. Brown writes him a federal-letterhead letter to carry as a hedge against extra-legal threats. Tuttle drives to Greenwood, Mississippi with Sam Block and Charles McLaurin and lodges at 708 Avenue N near the COFO office.

chapters.title: Medgar Evers's Murder and the Winona Beatings
chapters.summary: Lawrence Guyot leaves to bail Fannie Lou Hamer, June Johnson, and Annelle Ponder out of jail in Winona and is himself arrested and beaten. Tuttle calls Medgar Evers, who promises to call back after a meeting; Evers is murdered by Byron De La Beckwith that night. With Willie Wazir Peacock in charge, Tuttle calls Governor Ross Barnett with a scripted demand that produces the Highway Patrol's disclosure of where the prisoners are held. Andrew Young drives in with bail money.

chapters.title: Atlanta, Lester Maddox, and John Kifner Crosses the Line
chapters.summary: After De La Beckwith threatens to kill the white boy in Greenwood, Tuttle is flown to Atlanta. He participates in SNCC-led lunch-counter sit-ins outside the local civil rights consensus, observing Lester Maddox with a pickaxe handle outside his restaurant. New York Times reporter John Kifner, a Wesleyan acquaintance present at a near-boiling-water incident, puts away his notebook and joins the movement.

chapters.title: Savannah Night Marches and the House of Hospitality
chapters.summary: Tuttle travels to Savannah with Bruce Gordon and Joe Iyer to support Hosea Williams and Benjamin Van Clark's Chatham County Crusade for Voters. He participates in night marches where movement members surrender brass knuckles and switchblades before each action. The group is housed pro bono in a house of prostitution whose owners also put up property as bail collateral.

chapters.title: Chatham County Jail and the Bridge Boys
chapters.summary: Police arrest Tuttle in the Chatham County Crusade office on a John Doe warrant while Andrew Young and James Bevel ease away unrecognized. Tuttle is held under Judge Victor Mulling's seven-piece-bond detention scheme with Hosea Williams and roughly 45 others. Father Crowder visits to administer communion. African-American jail trustees called bridge boys, including a Filipino-American welterweight boxer, threaten white prisoners against touching him. Bruce Gordon and Tuttle organize a freedom school inside the jail.

chapters.title: Aaron Buchsbaum, William Waxler, and the Communist Smear
chapters.summary: Through an Anti-Defamation League network in Connecticut and a separate Cincinnati law-firm contact, attorney Aaron Buchsbaum agrees to represent Tuttle and Dr. William Waxler puts up property bond. The Atlanta Constitution runs a story linking the Chatham County Crusade office to the International Union of Mine, Mill, and Smelter Workers, framed to brand Tuttle a communist. California Attorney General Stanley Mosk and former Arkansas Governor Frank Cherry, then head of the Subversive Activities Control Board, intervene; the story dies. Tuttle's release is contingent on everyone else being released.

chapters.title: UCLA Student Coordinating Committee and No on Proposition 14
chapters.summary: Back in Los Angeles, Tuttle, Bob Singleton, and Russ Ellis form the UCLA Student Coordinating Committee. They mobilize over 100 students each weekend and 900 on Election Day for the No on Proposition 14 fair-housing campaign. The group functions as a Friends of SNCC chapter, lobbying Congressmen Augustus Hawkins, Phil Burton, and William Fitts Ryan to hold hearings on Mississippi. The chapter also covers the UC Berkeley Free Speech Movement connection and the RFK 1968 campaign in which Tuttle meets his first wife.

chapters.title: LA City Controller: Private Clubs and South Africa Divestment
chapters.summary: Tuttle serves four terms as Los Angeles City Controller beginning 1985. He leads passage of an ordinance prohibiting discrimination at the Jonathan Club, California Club, and Hillcrest Country Club, with Councilwoman Joy Picus. He requires sworn affidavits from city investment-holding companies to certify no South Africa business ties, an apartheid-divestment technique credited to Councilman Zev Yaroslavsky, Councilman Robert C. Farrell, and Mayor Tom Bradley. He extends the work to bank-merger redlining-disclosure ordinances.

chapters.title: Return to Greenwood, Savannah, and the Voting Rights Act
chapters.summary: Tuttle and his second wife Rebecca tour the civil rights sites in 2008-09, visiting 708 Avenue N in Greenwood, Canton, Philadelphia Mississippi, the former Chatham County Jail now part of the Savannah College of Art and Design, and a 1963 photo exhibit at the Telfair Museum featuring Tuttle's own quotes. An impromptu corner-store reunion in Greenwood with a former teenage SNCC volunteer closes the loop. Tuttle frames continuing work to defend the 1965 Voting Rights Act federal preclearance provisions and to extend civil rights principles to gender and sexual-orientation equity.
related_events: Shelby County v. Holder challenge to VRA Section 5

loc_video.caption: Rick Tuttle oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in Culver City, California, 2013 April 11
